the-entrance-hall: I wrote the velvet rope line before I had anything else, and the whole song built itself backward from that one prop. I wanted an opening track that wasn't an apology or an invitation but a set of house rules, delivered by someone who'd rehearsed being charming about her own boundaries. The alphabetizing detail is true, or true enough — I really do organize things when I'm avoiding a feeling. I almost cut the outro line about there being more rooms than I've shown anyone, because it felt like giving away the album's whole shape on track one. I kept it anyway. Some confessions you have to make before you've earned the right to.
the-mask-on-the-wall: This one started as a joke that stopped being funny halfway through writing it. I was going to write about literally owning a closet of party masks, something campy, and then the line about forgetting for an hour that I was wearing it showed up and I just sat with my hands off the keyboard for a while. The hairline seam behind the left ear is the detail I'm proudest of and most embarrassed by, because it came from an actual habit — I used to drop my face the second I got in the car. Eleven more in storage was originally fifty. I brought the number down because fifty sounded like exaggeration and eleven sounded like an inventory somebody actually keeps.
the-chair-arranged-for-visitors: I sat in an actual chair like this to write it — back straight, no cushion, facing a door nobody was going to open — just to feel how long I could hold it before my body argued with me. Not long. That's the whole song, really: the gap between the posture you can perform and the posture you can sustain. The cold glass detail almost got cut because a reviewer-in-my-head said it was too on-the-nose, glass chair, glass animal, very funny. I left it in because the literal thing was also just true — I own a chair like that and it actually is cold in July. Sometimes the symbol and the furniture are the same object and you don't get to pick.
the-photograph-they-chose-to-frame: I wrote this after finding an old contact sheet and counting the frames — there really were around forty, and I really only liked one. The line about my mother deciding what the story's going to be is the closest I've come to writing about her directly without using her name, and I left her unnamed on purpose, because the song isn't really an accusation, it's a confession that I picked up the same scissors. I almost ended on a hopeful note, promising to show the whole roll someday. I cut that promise down to 'maybe,' because I wasn't ready to make it a vow I'd have to keep by the next verse, let alone the next year.
hairline: The crack is real, or it was — a display case I own actually split along one edge, and I genuinely left it for a month deciding whether to fix it. I wrote the chorus before I knew if the song believed in fixing things or not, which is unusual for me; normally I know the ending before the bridge. The detail that someone noticed and didn't say anything is the one that still gets me — I added the chorus repeat after that verse because I wanted the music to flinch the same way I did. I left the outro unresolved on a half-finished chord because deciding to look isn't the same as deciding what you'll do with what you see.
the-mirror-turned-to-the-wall: I wrote the spoken verse last and almost didn't keep it spoken — I tried singing it three different ways and it kept sounding like performance, which was exactly the problem the verse was about. So I just talked it, dry, into the mic at 1am, and that take is the one on the record. The detail about turning it an inch and putting it back by morning happened roughly as written, except it was actually two inches and I left it three days, which felt like too long a confession even for a song about confessions. I shortened the timeline because the truth was slightly more pathetic than the song needed to be.
fingerprints-on-the-case: This is the only love song on the record that doesn't admit it's a love song until the chorus, and I planned it that way — I wanted the listener to think it was still about the museum's rules before it became about wanting someone to break them. The turned-down gallery lights are a real thing I do, dimming a room so I can keep looking at something a little longer without admitting that's what I'm doing. I wrote four different versions of the outro and kept the shortest, three words repeated, because the longer versions all tried to explain why I wasn't wiping it clean, and the explaining was exactly the performance I was trying to drop.
the-door-that-wont-lock: The broken lock is not a metaphor I invented for the song — I had an actual back door with an actual broken lock for the better part of a year, and the chair wedged under the handle is furniture I owned, not a lyric. Writing the spoken bridge was the first time I let myself admit, even just to a microphone, that some part of me wanted someone to walk in uninvited. I almost took that line out twice. It felt like handing over a key I hadn't decided I wanted to give away. I left it in because the song needed one sentence that was more honest than the singer, not the writer, was comfortable with.
static-between-exhibits: This track exists because I ran out of curator voice halfway through making the record and needed a place to put that down on the page. There's no chorus, no resolved chord, almost no rhythm, because I wanted one song where the architecture itself admits it doesn't know what it's doing. The spoken outro, about turning the lights back on in a minute, was unscripted — I left the talkback mic open after a take and that's the take we kept. I thought about cutting it for being too unfinished. Then I realized unfinished was the entire point of standing in a hallway between two rooms you built and trust neither of yet.
the-empty-pedestal: I'd avoided writing about the pedestal for the whole album and then it became the song I couldn't avoid anymore. The detail that the plaque has only a date and no title is true of how I actually think about turning nineteen — I don't have a name for who I was before, just a year I can point to. Writing 'I don't think she'd survive the rooms I built since' broke something open in the take we used; you can hear my voice catch on 'survive' if you listen close, and I left that imperfection in the mix instead of fixing it, because fixing it would have been exactly the kind of restoration the song is grieving.
the-drawer-of-discarded-photographs: I actually own this drawer. Not metaphorically — a real drawer, decades of photos I filed there the same day I took them because my own unguarded face embarrassed me. Writing the bridge where I apologize to a photograph felt absurd while I was doing it and then didn't feel absurd at all by the second take. The hospital waiting room photo is real too; I found it while writing this song, which is the only time on the record the writing and the discovery happened in the same week. I left the outro at 'I left the drawer open tonight' instead of writing a tidier ending, because the actual drawer is, as of this album, still open.
the-dress-that-no-longer-fits: I wrote this with an actual dress sitting on the chair next to me, which I hadn't worn in over a decade and had nonetheless kept folded like evidence. The line about preserving applause instead of a childhood took four drafts to get right because every earlier version sounded like I was blaming my grandmother, and that was never the target — the target was my own willingness to keep performing gratitude for a gift I'd outgrown. The bridge's small mercy, that I don't have to choose between honoring her and outgrowing the room, is the closest thing to advice I've ever let myself put in a song. I almost cut it for being too kind to myself. I left it because I'd earned one kind line by then.
the-unlocked-door: After three songs about a broken lock I owed the record an answer, so I wrote this one to force my own hand — I called an actual locksmith and asked him to remove the back door's lock entirely rather than fix it, and the line about him asking if I was sure is verbatim. The chorus took the least time to write of anything on the album, which worried me; usually the songs that come easy are the ones I distrust. I kept it anyway, because for once the ease felt like relief arriving on schedule rather than a song avoiding its own difficulty. The outro's 'still standing here, still whole' is the first unguarded brag I've allowed myself on this record.
the-object-with-no-label: The cup is real, it's chipped, and I drink from it every morning, which made it almost impossible to write a precious lyric about it — every draft sounded like I was dressing up a kitchen object in symbolism it hadn't asked for. The breakthrough was realizing the song's job was to refuse that dressing-up entirely, to let an ordinary thing stay ordinary on the record the way it stays ordinary on my shelf. The bridge line about not having to earn my place with a wound was the hardest sentence I've written for this album, not because of the words but because of how long it took me to believe it while singing it.
daylight-in-the-last-room: I wrote the closing track with the windows of my actual writing room open, which I almost never do, and I think the song knows it — there's a real draft audible under the vocal take if you listen with headphones, and I refused to fix it in the mix. I wanted to end the record without a final exhibit, without one more confession dressed as a museum piece, so the last verse has no object in it at all, just air and curtains and the most undefended 'me' I've put on any of these songs. The three-word outro took the longest to agree to, because ending on 'just me' meant admitting the tour was never really about the rooms.
